Adobe puts AI in everything, everywhere, all at once
The software giant is embedding AI into every corner of Creative Cloud, from Photoshop to Premiere to a new web-based video editor, while opening its tools to rival AI models from Google, OpenAI and others.
Written By:
Tom May
28 October 2025
Adobe is using its Max 2025 conference today to make a clear pitch. Instead of jumping between different AI tools, creatives should be able to access all the best AI software right inside the apps they already use... even if it isn't made by Adobe.
The simple message here is "choice". Adobe is opening up Photoshop's Generative Fill to use partner AI models from Google and Black Forest Labs, not just Adobe's own Firefly. It's a notable shift for a company that's spent the last two years pushing its proprietary AI.
For example, this means if you're retouching a photo in Photoshop, you can now pick which AI engine handles your "add a mountain in the background" request.
Adobe says this gives you more creative options, though it remains to be seen whether most people will care which model is doing the heavy lifting, or just want good results.
Video and AI assistants
Right now, video is the new frontier for AI. And so it's not surprising that at Adobe 2025, the company is making a serious play for creators who've been flocking to standalone AI tools.
Most strikingly, Firefly now generates background music that automatically syncs to the length of your footage. The demo I saw makes it seem very easy to use: the software analyses the video, determines the kind of mood you might be going for, and then suggests a range of options. There's also AI voice-over generation in multiple languages, and a new web-based video editor is coming.
For Premiere Pro users—the folks editing everything from YouTube videos to feature films— there's a new AI Object Mask tool that should speed up the tedious work of isolating people or objects for colour grading or effects. Anyone who's manually masked frame by frame will understand why this matters.
And that's not all. In a partnership that feels aimed squarely at the influencer economy, Adobe is building a direct pipeline from Premiere to YouTube Shorts. You'll be able to create short-form content in a new mobile tool and push it straight to YouTube, with templates designed specifically for the Shorts format.
This is Adobe acknowledging what's already happening: many Creative Cloud subscribers aren't making corporate videos or films any more. They're making content for social media, often as a business.
Something else Adobe's found hard to ignore is the rise of ChatGPT, which can now do things like image editing on request. To match this challenge, Adobe is rolling out conversational chatbots across its creative apps. In other words, instead of clicking through menus, you'll be able to tell Photoshop or Express what you want in plain English, and it'll execute the steps.
In Photoshop Web, for example, there's an assistant that can handle repetitive tasks; so you can ask it to do things like "remove the background from all 50 of these product photos." For creatives drowning in client revisions, that's the kind of automation that should really save a lot of time.
The future of creativity?
Adobe's survey data shows 86% of creators now use generative AI, and 76% say it's helped grow their business. Whether that's accurate or not, Adobe is clearly betting that the future of creative work involves AI at every step, from the initial idea to the final render.
The question is whether Adobe can convince people to do all of that inside Creative Cloud, rather than using Premiere Pro for video, Midjourney for images, and ElevenLabs for voice work, for instance. Today's announcements suggest Adobe knows it needs to compete by offering access to those other tools, not by trying to beat them.
A few things are missing from today's news, though. Pricing details for accessing partner AI models, and whether this AI buffet comes at an additional cost beyond existing Creative Cloud subscriptions. Also, how Adobe reconciles its "commercially safe" Firefly promise (trained only on licensed content) with offering access to partner models that may not have the same guardrails still appears to be handwaved away. For professionals worried about copyright, that distinction matters.
In terms of day-to-day work, though, the practical takeaway is this: the tools are getting faster at the boring, repetitive stuff. Theoretically, this means more time for the creative decisions only humans can make. In practice, many clients simply expect more revisions in the same timeframe. But as we can't put the genie back in the bottle, Adobe is at least helping its users keep up in this confusing new creative era.
